long_reaction_chains.name: Long Reaction Chains
long_reaction_chains.description: Multiple reaction steps where each step's product becomes the reactant for the next

structural_patterns: Product of one step becomes reactant of next

counting_enumeration.name: Counting/Enumeration
counting_enumeration.description: Questions asking 'how many' compounds satisfy a given property, or enumeration of specific cases

deep_mechanistic_reasoning.name: Deep Mechanistic Reasoning
deep_mechanistic_reasoning.description: Questions requiring understanding of reaction mechanisms beyond surface-level recall - intermediates, transition states, electron flow

comparative_ranking.name: Comparative Ranking
comparative_ranking.description: Questions requiring comparison of properties like acidity, basicity, electrophilicity, nucleophilicity, stability, bond strength, etc.

questions.question_text: 'Adsorption' principle is used for which of the following purification method? (1) Sublimation (2) Chromatography (3) Extraction (4) Distillation
questions.solution_summary: Adsorption chromatography is based on differential adsorption of components on adsorbent like silica gel or alumina. Sublimation, extraction and distillation do not use adsorption principle.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Recall of chromatography principle.

questions.question_text: The correct sequence of electron gain enthalpy of the elements listed below is: A. Ar B. Br C. F D. S
questions.solution_summary: Electron gain enthalpy (kJ/mol): Ar = +96 (endothermic), S = -200, Br = -325, F = -333. More negative = more exothermic. Order: Ar > S > Br > F.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Recall of electron gain enthalpy values and comparison.

questions.question_text: The compound that is white in colour is: (1) ammonium arsinomolybdate (2) lead sulphate (3) ammonium sulphide (4) lead iodide
questions.solution_summary: Lead sulphate (PbSO₄) is a white solid. Ammonium arsinomolybdate is yellow, ammonium sulphide is yellow, lead iodide is yellow.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Recall of colours of inorganic compounds.

questions.question_text: The linear combination of atomic orbitals to form molecular orbitals takes place only when the combining atomic orbitals: A. have the same energy B. have the minimum overlap C. have same symmetry about the molecular axis D. have different symmetry about the molecular axis.
questions.sub_topic: Molecular orbital theory - conditions for LCAO
questions.solution_summary: For LCAO to form MOs: orbitals must have same energy (A), have maximum overlap (not minimum - B false), and have same symmetry about the molecular axis (C true, D false). So only A and C are correct.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires understanding of LCAO conditions.

questions.question_text: Consider the oxides of group 14 elements: SiO₂, GeO₂, SnO₂, PbO₂, CO and GeO. The amphoteric oxides are: (1) SnO₂, CO (2) SiO₂, GeO₂ (3) GeO, GeO₂ (4) SnO₂, PbO₂
questions.solution_summary: SnO₂ and PbO₂ are amphoteric oxides. CO is neutral. SiO₂, GeO₂ are acidic. GeO is amphoteric but among the options, SnO₂ and PbO₂ is correct.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires knowledge of acid-base nature of group 14 oxides.

questions.question_text: The metals that are employed in the battery industries are: A. Fe B. Mn C. Ni D. Cr E. Cd
questions.solution_summary: Mn (dry cell), Ni (Ni-Cd cell), Cd (Ni-Cd cell) are used in battery industries. Fe and Cr are not typically used in batteries.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Recall of metals used in battery industry.

questions.question_text: Match List I with List II: List I: Glucose/NaHCO₃/Δ, Glucose/HNO₃, Glucose/HI/Δ, Glucose/Bromine water. List II: Gluconic acid, No reaction, n-hexane, Saccharic acid.
questions.solution_summary: Glucose + NaHCO₃ → No reaction (A-II); Glucose + HNO₃ → Saccharic acid (B-IV); Glucose + HI/Δ → n-Hexane (C-III); Glucose + Br₂ water → Gluconic acid (D-I).

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Matching of glucose reactions with products.

questions.question_text: The product (C) in the below mentioned reaction is: CH₃-CH₂-CH₂-Br → KOH(alc) → A → HBr → B → KOH(aq)/Δ → C
questions.solution_summary: CH₃CH₂CH₂Br + alc. KOH → propene (A). Propene + HBr → 2-bromopropane (B). 2-bromopropane + aq. KOH → propan-2-ol (C).

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Multi-step reaction sequence involving elimination and addition.

questions.question_text: Identify correct statements from below: A. The chromate ion is square planar. B. Dichromates are generally prepared from chromates. C. The green manganate ion is diamagnetic. D. Dark green coloured K₂MnO₄ disproportionates in a neutral or acidic medium to give permanganate. E. With increasing oxidation number of transition metal, ionic character of the oxides decreases.
questions.solution_summary: A: False (chromate is tetrahedral). B: True (dichromates are prepared from chromates). C: False (manganate MnO₄²⁻ has 1 unpaired electron, paramagnetic). D: True (K₂MnO₄ disproportionates in neutral/acidic medium). E: True (higher oxidation state oxides are more covalent).

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires knowledge of chromates, manganates and oxide properties.

questions.question_text: The correct statements from following are: A. The strength of anionic ligands can be explained by crystal field theory. B. Valence bond theory does not give a quantitative interpretation of kinetic stability of coordination compounds. C. The hybridisation involved in formation of [Ni(CN)₄]²⁻ complex is dsp². D. The number of possible isomer(s) of cis-[PtCl₂(en)₂]²⁺ is one.
questions.solution_summary: A: False (LFT explains ligand strength, not CFT). B: True (VBT doesn't give quantitative kinetic stability). C: True ([Ni(CN)₄]²⁻ is dsp²). D: False (cis-[PtCl₂(en)₂]²⁺ has two isomers - enantiomers).

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires knowledge of VBT, CFT and isomerism in coordination compounds.

questions.question_text: For the given reaction, choose the correct expression of Kc from the following: Fe³⁺(aq) + SCN⁻(aq) ⇌ (FeSCN)²⁺(aq)
questions.solution_summary: Kc = [FeSCN²⁺] / ([Fe³⁺][SCN⁻]). The expression uses equilibrium concentrations of products over reactants.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Recall of equilibrium constant expression.

questions.question_text: Integrated rate law equation for a first order gas phase reaction is given by (where P_i is initial pressure and P_t is total pressure at time t):
questions.solution_summary: For A(g) → 2B(g), k = (2.303/t)log(P_i/(2P_i - P_t)). This is the integrated rate law for first order gas phase reaction.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires understanding of first order kinetics in gas phase.

questions.question_text: Match List I with List II: List I (Technique): Distillation, Fractional distillation, Steam distillation, Distillation under reduced pressure. List II (Application): Separation of glycerol from spent-lye, Aniline-Water mixture, Separation of crude oil fractions, Chloroform-Aniline.
questions.solution_summary: Distillation → Chloroform-Aniline (A-IV); Fractional distillation → Separation of crude oil fractions (B-III); Steam distillation → Aniline-Water mixture (C-II); Distillation under reduced pressure → Separation of glycerol from spent-lye (D-I).

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Matching of distillation techniques with applications.

questions.question_text: Identify the mixture that shows positive deviations from Raoult's law: (1) (CH₃)₂CO + C₆H₅NH₂ (2) CHCl₃ + C₆H₆ (3) CHCl₃ + (CH₃)₂CO (4) (CH₃)₂CO + CS₂
questions.solution_summary: Acetone (CH₃)₂CO + CS₂ shows positive deviation from Raoult's law because intermolecular forces between unlike molecules are weaker than those between like molecules.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires knowledge of non-ideal solutions and deviations from Raoult's law.

questions.question_text: Given below are two statements: Assertion A: Alcohols react both as nucleophiles and electrophiles. Reason R: Alcohol is react with active metals such as sodium, potassium and aluminium to yield corresponding alkoxides and liberate hydrogen.
questions.solution_summary: Assertion (A) is true: Alcohols can act as nucleophiles (via O-H) and as electrophiles (via C-O bond cleavage). Reason (R) is true but doesn't explain A (R explains acidic nature of alcohols, not dual behaviour).

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires understanding of alcohol reactivity as both nucleophile and electrophile.

questions.question_text: Given below are two statements: Assertion A: pKₐ value of phenol is 10.0 while that of ethanol is 15.9. Reason R: Ethanol is stronger acid than phenol.
questions.solution_summary: Assertion (A) is true: pKₐ phenol = 10.0, ethanol = 15.9. Reason (R) is false: Lower pKₐ means stronger acid. Phenol is stronger acid (pKₐ 10.0) than ethanol (pKₐ 15.9).

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires comparison of acidity based on pKₐ values.

questions.question_text: A species having carbon with sextet of electrons and can act as electrophile is called: (1) Pentavalent carbon (2) Carbon free radical (3) Carbocation (4) Carbanion
questions.solution_summary: Carbocation has carbon with only 6 electrons (sextet) in its valence shell, making it electron deficient and an electrophile.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Recall of carbocation definition.

questions.question_text: Given below are two statements: Statement I: IUPAC name of HO-CH₂-(CH₂)₃-CH₂-COCH₃ is 7-hydroxyheptan-2-one. Statement II: 2-oxoheptan-7-ol is the correct IUPAC name for above compound.
questions.sub_topic: IUPAC nomenclature - ketones with OH substituent
questions.solution_summary: The compound is 7-hydroxyheptan-2-one (ketone gets lower locant than alcohol as per IUPAC priority). Statement I is correct, Statement II is incorrect.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires IUPAC nomenclature with multiple functional groups.

questions.question_text: Given below are two statements: Statement I: Noble gases have very high boiling points. Statement II: Noble gases are monoatomic gases. They are held together by strong dispersion forces. Because of this they are liquefied at very low temperature. Hence, they have very high boiling points.
questions.solution_summary: Statement I is false: Noble gases have very low boiling points. Statement II is also false: Noble gases have weak dispersion forces (not strong), hence very low boiling points.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Factual recall of noble gas properties.

questions.question_text: Identify the factor from the following that does not affect electrolytic conductance of a solution: (1) The nature of solvent used (2) Concentration of the electrolyte (3) The nature of the electrolyte added (4) The nature of the electrode used
questions.solution_summary: Electrolytic conductance depends on: nature of electrolyte, concentration, nature of solvent, temperature. It does NOT depend on the nature of the electrode used (electrode affects measurement, not the solution's conductance).

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Recall of factors affecting electrolytic conductance.

questions.question_text: The 'spin only' magnetic moment for [Ni(NH₃)₆]²⁺ is ____ × 10⁻¹ BM (given atomic number of Ni: 28)
questions.solution_summary: Ni²⁺ in [Ni(NH₃)₆]²⁺ has d⁸ configuration with 2 unpaired electrons. μ = √(2(2+2)) = √8 = 2.82 BM = 28.2 × 10⁻¹ BM ≈ 28 × 10⁻¹ BM.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Magnetic moment calculation from d-electron configuration.

questions.question_text: Number of moles of methane required to produce 22 g CO₂(g) after combustion is ____ × 10⁻² moles. The value of x is ____.
questions.solution_summary: CH₄ + 2O₂ → CO₂ + 2H₂O. 1 mol CH₄ gives 1 mol CO₂ (44 g). For 22 g CO₂: moles = 22/44 = 0.5 mol = 50 × 10⁻² mol.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Stoichiometric calculation from combustion reaction.

questions.question_text: Number of alkanes obtained on electrolysis of a mixture of CH₃COONa and C₂H₅COONa is ____.
questions.solution_summary: Kolbe's electrolysis of mixed salts gives: ethane (CH₃-CH₃), propane (CH₃-CH₂-CH₃), and butane (CH₃CH₂-CH₂CH₃). Total 3 alkanes.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires knowledge of Kolbe's electrolysis products.

questions.question_text: Molar mass of the salt from NaBr, NaNO₃, KI and CaF₂ which does not evolve coloured vapours on heating with concentrated H₂SO₄ is ____ g mol⁻¹. (Molar mass in g mol⁻¹: Na:23, N:14, K:39, O:16, Br:80, I:127, F:19, Ca:40)
questions.sub_topic: Reaction with conc. H₂SO₄ - coloured vapours
questions.solution_summary: CaF₂ + H₂SO₄ → CaSO₄ + 2HF (no coloured vapour). Molar mass of CaF₂ = 40 + 2(19) = 78 g/mol. Other salts give coloured vapours (Br₂, NO₂, I₂).

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires knowledge of reaction of halides with conc. H₂SO₄.

questions.question_text: The product of the following reaction has number of hydroxyl groups = ____.
questions.solution_summary: The product has one phenolic -OH group. Other hydroxyl groups are consumed in the reaction.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires product analysis to count hydroxyl groups.

questions.question_text: The ionization energy of sodium in kJ mol⁻¹, if electromagnetic radiation of wavelength 242 nm is just sufficient to ionize sodium atom is ____.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Calculation of ionisation energy from wavelength using Planck's equation.

questions.question_text: The number of species from the following in which the central atom uses sp³ hybrid orbitals in its bonding is: NH₃, SO₂, SiO₂, BeCl₂, CO₂, H₂O, CH₄, BF₃
questions.solution_summary: sp³ hybridised species: NH₃ (4 electron pairs), H₂O (4 electron pairs), CH₄ (4 bond pairs). SiO₂ has extended structure. SO₂ is sp², BeCl₂ is sp, CO₂ is sp, BF₃ is sp². Count = 3? Actually NH₃, H₂O, CH₄ = 3. But the answer is 4 - including SiO₂? Wait, SiO₂ is not sp³. Actually NH₃, H₂O, CH₄ = 3 species. Let me check: NH₃ (sp³), H₂O (sp³), CH₄ (sp³) = 3.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires identification of hybridisation for multiple species.

questions.question_text: The product P of the following reaction is: The number of hydroxyl groups present in the product P is ____.
questions.solution_summary: The product has zero hydroxyl groups as all -OH groups are consumed in the reaction.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Requires product analysis to count hydroxyl groups.

questions.question_text: The rate of first order reaction is 0.04 mol L⁻¹ s⁻¹ at 10 minutes and 0.03 mol L⁻¹ s⁻¹ at 20 minutes after initiation. Half life of the reaction is ____ minutes.
questions.sub_topic: First order kinetics - rate and half-life

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Application of first order kinetics to determine rate constant and half-life.

questions.question_text: The pH at which Mg(OH)₂ [Ksp = 1×10⁻¹¹] begins to precipitate from a solution containing 0.10 M Mg²⁺ is ____.

archetype_analysis.reasoning: Application of Ksp to find pH of precipitation.
